title: Covalent bonding — sharing electrons to fill shells
examTip: AQA Paper 1, Topic 2 (Bonding, Structure and Properties of Matter). Expect questions on dot-and-cross diagrams, explaining properties of simple vs giant covalent structures, and comparing structure types.
introTips: Covers simple covalent molecules and giant covalent structures
You'll be able to draw dot-and-cross diagrams and explain properties from structure
By the end you can compare covalent, ionic and metallic bonding in terms of melting point and conductivity
intro: Most of the molecules that make up living things — water, oxygen, glucose — are held together by covalent bonds. Understanding covalent bonding is the key to explaining why water is a liquid at room temperature, why diamond is the hardest natural substance, and why graphite conducts electricity. AQA loves asking you to link structure to properties, so getting this right unlocks marks across multiple question types.

keyPoints.heading: Why atoms form covalent bonds
keyPoints.content: Atoms form covalent bonds because they are trying to achieve a full outer electron shell, which gives them the stable electronic structure of a noble gas. Instead of transferring electrons (as in ionic bonding), non-metal atoms share pairs of electrons. Each shared pair counts as one covalent bond. The shared electrons are attracted to the nuclei of both atoms simultaneously — this electrostatic attraction between the nuclei and the shared pair is what holds the atoms together. A single bond involves one shared pair, a double bond involves two shared pairs.
writeBullets: Covalent bond — a shared pair of electrons between two non-metal atoms
Driving force — each atom achieves a full outer shell (noble gas configuration)
Single bond — one shared pair of electrons
Double bond — two shared pairs of electrons
Electrostatic attraction — both nuclei attracted to the shared electrons, holding atoms together

keyTerms.def: A shared pair of electrons between two non-metal atoms
One shared pair of electrons between two atoms
Two shared pairs of electrons between two atoms

keyPoints.memTip: Think of two people sharing an umbrella — both stay dry (both fill their shells) because they share. Cover your notes and write all 5 bullets from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say atoms 'want' electrons — that's fine for understanding, but in the exam say 'to achieve a full outer shell' or 'to gain the electronic structure of a noble gas'. That's the language that gets marks.

keyPoints.heading: Simple covalent molecules — dot-and-cross diagrams
keyPoints.content: Simple covalent molecules contain a small, fixed number of atoms joined by covalent bonds. You need to know the structures of: H₂ (1 shared pair, each H achieves 2 electrons), Cl₂ (1 shared pair, each Cl achieves 8), O₂ (double bond — 2 shared pairs, each O achieves 8), HCl (1 shared pair), H₂O (2 single bonds, O has 2 lone pairs), NH₃ (3 single bonds, N has 1 lone pair), and CH₄ (4 single bonds, C has no lone pairs). In a dot-and-cross diagram, draw the outer shell electrons of each atom, using dots for one atom and crosses for the other. Show shared pairs in the overlap region between the two atoms.
writeBullets: H₂ — 1 single bond, each hydrogen has 2 electrons (full shell)
O₂ — 1 double bond, each oxygen has 8 outer electrons
H₂O — 2 single bonds, oxygen has 2 lone pairs
NH₃ — 3 single bonds, nitrogen has 1 lone pair
CH₄ — 4 single bonds, carbon has no lone pairs
Dot-and-cross — show shared pairs in the overlap; lone pairs outside

keyTerms.def: A small number of atoms joined by covalent bonds, with weak forces between molecules
A diagram showing the outer shell electrons of each atom, with shared pairs in an overlap region
A pair of electrons in an atom's outer shell that is not involved in bonding

keyPoints.memTip: HONCHO: H₂O, O₂, N₂, CH₄, Cl₂, HCl, O₂ — tick these off when drawing dot-and-cross. Cover your notes and draw the dot-and-cross for H₂O and NH₃ from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The most common error is forgetting lone pairs. In H₂O, oxygen has two lone pairs — you must draw them or you lose marks. Also, only show the outer shell electrons, not all electrons.

keyPoints.heading: Properties of simple covalent substances
keyPoints.content: Simple molecular substances have low melting and boiling points because the intermolecular forces (forces between molecules) are weak. Note: the covalent bonds within each molecule are strong — but it is the weak intermolecular forces that are broken when the substance melts or boils, not the bonds. This is why most simple covalent substances are gases or liquids at room temperature (e.g. oxygen, water, ammonia). They do not conduct electricity because they have no free ions or free electrons — all electrons are held within covalent bonds. They are generally insoluble in water but may dissolve in organic solvents.
writeBullets: Low melting/boiling points — weak intermolecular forces broken on melting, not the covalent bonds
Gas or liquid at RTP — most simple molecules e.g. O₂, H₂O, NH₃
No electrical conductivity — no free ions or free electrons
Intermolecular forces — weak forces between molecules (not within them)
Covalent bonds — strong within molecules but irrelevant to melting point

keyTerms.def: Weak forces of attraction between molecules (not the covalent bonds within them)

keyPoints.memTip: Key phrase: 'It is the weak intermolecular forces that are broken, NOT the covalent bonds.' Say this out loud three times, then cover and write it from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is one of the most common 3-mark mistakes in Paper 1. Students say 'covalent bonds break when it melts' — wrong. It is the intermolecular forces that break. The covalent bonds stay intact. AQA specifically tests this.

keyPoints.heading: Giant covalent structures — diamond, graphite, silicon dioxide
keyPoints.content: Giant covalent structures (also called macromolecular structures) contain millions of atoms all joined by strong covalent bonds in a continuous lattice — there are no separate molecules. This gives them very high melting and boiling points because a huge number of strong covalent bonds must be broken. Diamond: each carbon atom forms 4 covalent bonds in a tetrahedral arrangement. Extremely hard, does not conduct electricity (no free electrons). Graphite: each carbon forms 3 covalent bonds, arranged in hexagonal layers. The fourth electron from each carbon is delocalised between layers — this makes graphite a conductor of electricity and a good lubricant (layers slide over each other). Silicon dioxide (SiO₂): each silicon is bonded to 4 oxygens in a giant lattice. Very high melting point, does not conduct electricity.
writeBullets: Giant covalent — millions of atoms in a continuous covalent lattice, no separate molecules
Very high melting points — vast numbers of strong covalent bonds must be broken
Diamond — each C bonds to 4 others (tetrahedral); very hard; no electrical conductivity
Graphite — each C bonds to 3 others; layered hexagons; delocalised electrons conduct electricity
Graphite layers — slide over each other due to weak forces between layers (lubricant)
Silicon dioxide — Si bonded to 4 O atoms; giant lattice; no conductivity

keyTerms.def: A lattice of millions of atoms all joined by covalent bonds, with no separate molecules
Electrons not held by any one atom, free to move through a structure — enables electrical conductivity
Allotrope of carbon where each atom bonds to 4 others tetrahedrally; very hard, no conductivity
Allotrope of carbon with layered hexagonal sheets; conducts electricity due to delocalised electrons

keyPoints.memTip: Diamond = 4 bonds = no conductivity. Graphite = 3 bonds + 1 free electron = conducts. Cover and write both from memory with the reason.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Graphite conducting electricity is the key exception to learn — most covalent structures do not conduct. AQA often asks you to explain WHY graphite conducts. Always say 'delocalised electrons that are free to move and carry charge' — both parts needed for full marks.

0.question: Why do atoms form covalent bonds rather than ionic bonds?
options: To lose electrons and form positive ions
To gain electrons from a metal atom
To share electron pairs and achieve a full outer shell
To transfer electrons to achieve a noble gas structure
0.correctFeedback: Correct — covalent bonding involves sharing electron pairs so both atoms achieve a full outer shell.
0.wrongFeedback: Covalent bonding is all about sharing, not transferring. Both atoms share a pair of electrons, and in doing so both achieve a full outer shell — just like a noble gas. Ionic bonding involves electron transfer; covalent bonding involves sharing.

2.question: Why do simple covalent substances have low melting points?
options: Because their covalent bonds are weak and easy to break
Because the weak intermolecular forces between molecules require little energy to overcome
Because they are always gases at room temperature
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right — it is the weak intermolecular forces between molecules that break on melting, not the covalent bonds.
2.wrongFeedback: The covalent bonds within a simple molecule are actually strong — but they don't break when the substance melts. What breaks are the weak intermolecular forces between separate molecules. That's why simple covalent substances have low melting points. This is a very common exam mistake.

3.question: Which statement correctly explains why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not?
options: Graphite has ionic bonds; diamond does not
Graphite has delocalised electrons free to move; diamond has all electrons held in covalent bonds
Diamond has a higher melting point than graphite
Graphite dissolves in water, releasing ions
3.correctFeedback: Correct — in graphite each carbon only forms 3 bonds, leaving one electron per atom delocalised and free to carry charge. In diamond all 4 outer electrons are used in bonds.
3.wrongFeedback: The key is delocalised electrons. In graphite, each carbon forms only 3 covalent bonds. The fourth outer electron is delocalised — free to move through the structure and carry charge. In diamond, all 4 outer electrons are locked in covalent bonds, so there are no free charges to conduct electricity.

commonMistakes: Saying 'covalent bonds break when a simple molecule melts' — it is the weak intermolecular forces that break, not the bonds within the molecule.
Forgetting lone pairs in dot-and-cross diagrams — H₂O has 2 lone pairs on oxygen, NH₃ has 1 lone pair on nitrogen.
Saying graphite doesn't conduct electricity — it does, because of delocalised electrons. It is the exception among covalent structures.
Confusing giant covalent structures with ionic lattices — giant covalent structures have no ions, only covalently bonded atoms.
Drawing all electrons (not just outer shell electrons) in dot-and-cross diagrams — only show the outer shell.
examTips: When asked why a simple covalent substance has a low melting point, always write 'weak intermolecular forces' — not 'weak bonds'. The bonds are strong; the forces between molecules are weak.
For dot-and-cross questions, use dots for one atom and crosses for the other, show lone pairs clearly, and only draw outer shell electrons.
When explaining why graphite conducts electricity, include both parts: 'delocalised electrons' AND 'free to move and carry charge'.
In comparison questions, remember that ionic compounds conduct when molten or dissolved (ions free to move) but not as solids — giant covalent structures (except graphite) never conduct.
revisionCardBullets: Covalent bond — a shared pair of electrons between two non-metal atoms
Atoms share electrons to achieve a full outer shell (noble gas configuration)
Simple molecules have low melting points — weak intermolecular forces broken (NOT covalent bonds)
Simple covalent substances do not conduct electricity — no free ions or electrons
Giant covalent structures — millions of atoms bonded in a continuous lattice; very high melting points
Diamond — each C bonds to 4 others; very hard; no electrical conductivity
Graphite — each C bonds to 3 others; delocalised electrons allow conductivity; layers slide (lubricant)
Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) — giant covalent lattice; very high melting point; no conductivity
H₂O — 2 single bonds, 2 lone pairs on O
NH₃ — 3 single bonds, 1 lone pair on N
CH₄ — 4 single bonds, no lone pairs on C
O₂ — 1 double bond (2 shared pairs) between 2 oxygen atoms
